coredata.dc:title: High-Frequency Negative Capacitance in Graphene Quantum Dots/Lanthanum(III) Hydroxide-based MIS Heterostructure
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Anter, Aslıhan
Ulusoy, Murat
Polat, Barış
Yıldız, Mustafa
Di Bartolomeo, Antonio
Bi, Jinshun
Orhan, Elif
coredata.dc:description: Lanthanides have significant potential for electronic technologies based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as they have unique electronic configurations characterized by 4f electrons. In this context, lanthanum(III) hydroxide nanoparticles (La(OH)3NPs) are used as dopants for polyethyleneimine (PEI)-doped nitrogen (N)-doped graphene quantum dots(PEIGQDsN) in this study. Using a novel green method, the La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites are prepared from La(NO)3 in a single step and exploited as an interlayer in a metal/interlayer/semiconductor (MIS) heterojunction with Au and n-Si. Capacitance & conductance-voltage (C-V & G/ω-V) characteristics of the Au/La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN/n-Si MIS heterojunction have been investigated as a function of frequency in the wide 500 Hz to 3 MHz range from −3 V to 5 V, at 300 K. It has been observed that the structure is highly sensitive to the frequency. In particular, at high frequencies, above 1.5 MHz, the positive capacitance (PC) transforms into a negative capacitance (NC) in forward bias. In addition, impedance measurements at high frequencies were carried out after the measurements in the dark, while the surface of the structure was illuminated at 100 mW/cm2. At the frequencies of 2 MHz and 3 MHz, where inductive behavior was observed, the light refilled the depleted trap levels, catalyzing the transition from NC to PC in forward bias. These findings suggest that the capacitance and conductance of the heterojunction have a remarkable frequency sensitivity, particularly evident at higher frequencies. The outcomes of this study are poised to significantly influence the comprehension of carbon-lanthanides-based electronic technology, and enable the creation of new hybrid functional materials for use in electronic or optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanum (III) hydroxide nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Green method
Nanocomposite diode
Negative/positive capacitance behaviors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PEI-functionalized N-doped GQDs ( PEIGQDsN ) and La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites
Characterization of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN by FT-IR, UV–Vis, TEM, and XPS

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Scientific Research Council of Gazi University (BAP) grant number FGA-2022\u20138252, the REACT-EU PON Research and Innovation 2014\u20132020 project of the European Union, the Italian Ministerial Decree 1062/2021 and the University of Salerno, grant number ORSA235199.
The authors acknowledge Prof. M. Passacantando and Dr. E. Faella from the University of L'Aquila, Italy, for the XPS characterization of the interlayer materials used in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) C–V curves at low and medium frequencies, (b) C–V curves at high frequencies. (c) G/ω-V and (d) Ri -V curves at all frequencies for the Au/La(OH)3NPs-PEIGQDsN/n-Si heterojunction.
(a) The C and G/ω − V curves for capacitive behavior at 1 MHz. (b) C and G/ω − V curves for inductive behavior at 3 MHz.
(a-b) Gp /ω vs. lnf for different voltage regions, and (c-d) Nss − τ vs. V curves corresponding to these voltage regions of the Au/La(OH)3NPs-PEIGQDsN/n-Si heterojunction, respectively.
(a-b) The C and Gp /ω vs. V plots at 1 MHz, and (c-d) C and Gp /ω vs. V plots at 3 MHz in the dark and under illumination.
Light-induced charge (a) recombination process for PC at 1 MHz, (b) generation process for NC at 3 MHz.
Synthesis of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites.
The basic electrical parameters of the Au/La(OH)3NPs-PEIGQDsN/n-Si heterojunction.
Comparison of the NC-PC behaviors of doped and undoped PEIGQDsN structures.

row.entry:
NC
at low frequencies
(1 to 5 kHz)

From PC to NC at high frequencies (above 1.5 MHz)
From NC to PC at high frequencies
(2 MHz and 3 MHz) under illumination

head.ce:title: High-Frequency Negative Capacitance in Graphene Quantum Dots/Lanthanum(III) Hydroxide-based MIS Heterostructure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chinese Academy Science (CAS), Institute of Microelectronics, 10010 Beijing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chinese Academy Science (CAS), Institute of Microelectronics, 10010 Beijing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: These findings are poised to significantly advance the understanding of carbon-lanthanides-based electronics technology.

Lanthanides have significant potential for electronic technologies based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as they have unique electronic configurations characterized by 4f electrons. In this context, lanthanum(III) hydroxide nanoparticles (La(OH)3NPs) are used as dopants for polyethyleneimine (PEI)-doped nitrogen (N)-doped graphene quantum dots(PEIGQDsN) in this study. Using a novel green method, the La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites are prepared from La(NO)3 in a single step and exploited as an interlayer in a metal/interlayer/semiconductor (MIS) heterojunction with Au and n-Si. Capacitance & conductance-voltage (C-V & G/ω-V) characteristics of the Au/La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN/n-Si MIS heterojunction have been investigated as a function of frequency in the wide 500 Hz to 3 MHz range from −3 V to 5 V, at 300 K. It has been observed that the structure is highly sensitive to the frequency. In particular, at high frequencies, above 1.5 MHz, the positive capacitance (PC) transforms into a negative capacitance (NC) in forward bias. In addition, impedance measurements at high frequencies were carried out after the measurements in the dark, while the surface of the structure was illuminated at 100 mW/cm2. At the frequencies of 2 MHz and 3 MHz, where inductive behavior was observed, the light refilled the depleted trap levels, catalyzing the transition from NC to PC in forward bias. These findings suggest that the capacitance and conductance of the heterojunction have a remarkable frequency sensitivity, particularly evident at higher frequencies. The outcomes of this study are poised to significantly influence the comprehension of carbon-lanthanides-based electronic technology, and enable the creation of new hybrid functional materials for use in electronic or optoelectronic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as an important family of nanomaterials for their excellent optical, electrical, electrochemical, and biomedical properties resulting from their unique quantum confinement effects [1–3]. Owing to their easy functionalization, GQDs can be used in a variety of applications. For instance, GQDs with nitrogen doping (N-GQDs) produce blue light and exhibit electrocatalytic performance in the oxygen reduction reaction under alkaline conditions [4,5].

The type of amines that are used as nitrogen dopants can further tailor the properties of GQDs for specific applications. In this study, we choose polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a nitrogen dopant because it exhibits unique and versatile properties, such as solubility in water and many organic solvents, good thermal stability, low toxicity, excellent adhesive properties, and positive charge transport [5–10].

Considering that lanthanides are widely used as dopants in optoelectronics to improve the physical and optical properties of semiconductor devices, there is a notable surge in research focusing on doping carbon-based materials like GQDs with lanthanides to create novel hybrid functional materials for electronic or optoelectronic applications [11–13]. Lanthanum (La), characterized by electronic configuration featuring 4f electrons, has been synthesized in various compositions such as La(OH)3 [14], La2O3 [15], La2(CO3)3 [16], LaOF [17], LaF3 [18], LaPO4 [19], LaBO3 [20] nanoparticles using different synthesis methods [21–27]. As lanthanides and PEIGQDsN alone show very interesting electronic and optoelectronic properties, it is expected that nanocomposites formed by both materials exhibit even more interesting properties, which have not been explored yet. In a recent work [9], the electrical properties of such nanocomposite by using it in a metal-interlayer-semiconductor (MIS) heterostructure was investigated at first time. According to the results, the diode showed a high rectification and promising optoelectronic properties. In this work, we focus on a less investigated aspect of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN , specifically their capacitance properties, to better understand the overall characteristics of this nanocomposite.

Capacitance characteristics play a crucial role in electronic applications. Capacitors, which are essential components in electronic devices, typically demonstrate positive capacitance (PC) behavior. However, there are instances where negative capacitance (NC) behavior may occur. NC indicates that the charge on the electrode will be reduced by an increase in the bias voltage. NC indicates inductivity [28]. Some researchers have also reported NC behavior in Schottky diodes [29,30]. Berktaş et al. were the first to report the NC behavior at low frequencies of PEI-functionalized nitrogen-doped GQDs [6,7].
On the other hand, Orhan et al. constructed diodes utilizing PEI-functionalized nitrogen-doped GQDs doped with gadolinium (Gd), without observing a an NC behavior [8].

The main objective is to investigate the effect of the doping of GQDs with lanthanum, an element from the lanthanide series, on their C-V & G/ω-V characteristics. For the first time in the literature, the C-V & G/ω-V characteristics of the Au/La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN/n-Si MIS heterojunction are investigated as a function of frequency in the wide 500 Hz to 3 MHz range from −3V to 5V at 300 K. Remarkably, herein we report and discuss a PC to NC transition at high frequency in the fabricated diode at first time. This pioneering study provides valuable insights into the frequency-dependent behavior of carbon-lanthanide-based electronic materials and enables the creation of new hybrid functional materials for use in electronic or optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PEI-functionalized N-doped GQDs ( PEIGQDsN ) and La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The citric acid (CA), La(NO3)3·6H2O, and polyethyleneimine (PEI) (Mw: 1300, 50 wt% in H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were used as supplied. PEIGQDsN were synthesized in a single step by a hydrothermal process as given in our previous studies [6–9]. A solution of citric acid (1.80 g) and PEI (3.71 g) in 50 mL of deionized water was placed in a Teflon container in an autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 18 h. Then, to prepare La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites, a solution of PEIGQDsN (1 g) in 100 mL deionized water and 20 mL 0.1 M La(NO3)3·6H2O solution was added to a 250 mL round bottom flask. The mixture was heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 2h, then cooled to room temperature, and the resulting La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites were washed once with deionized distilled water and ethanol (Scheme 1 ).

To fabricate the Au/La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN/n-Si heterostructure, we utilized n-type Si (1–10 Ωcm, 350 μm thickness) wafers (100). To establish an ohmic contact, a layer of Au (99.999% pure) with a thickness of 150 nm was deposited onto the scratched back-side of the n-Si wafer using sputtering techniques. Following that, a spin-coating method was utilized to coat the polished surface of the wafer with the La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposite solution. The spinning speed was adjusted to 3000 rpm, and the spinning process was maintained for 30 s, resulting in the formation of a thin film with a thickness of 30 nm. Finally, circular Au contacts, 150 nm in thickness, were sputtered onto the La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN n-Si, using a circular metal mask with a diameter of 0.5 mm. Fig. 1 illustrates the structure of the Au/La(OH)3NPs-doped PEIGQDsN /n-Si heterojunction structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN by FT-IR, UV–Vis, TEM, and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN was fully characterized by FT-IR, UV–Vis, TEM, and XPS [9]. In the FT-IR spectrum of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites. A previous study9 provided details of the characterisations. In FT-IR spectra, a new vibrational frequency, which was not observed in PEIGQDsN , provides evidence of La-O bond formation in the nanocomposite [9]. These data indicate the conversion of lanthanum (III)' nitrate to La(OH)3. The UV–Vis spectra of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites were analyzed in water [9]. A band assigned to n-π* transitions of C=O and C=N is observed at 355 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum of La(OH)3NPs doped PEI-NGQDs nanocomposites and at 345 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum of PEIGQDsN nanocomposites. Pure La(OH)3 absorption peak was observed at around 250 nm [31]. TEM analysis of La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposites shows that the La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN in the nanocomposite have a spherical structure with an average size of 9–27 nm. In the XPS spectra, the atomic percentage of C, O, N, and La in the La(OH)3NPs/PEIGQDsN nanocomposites are 68%, 24%, 7%, and 1%, respectively. XPS spectra were interpreted in detail in our previous study9.

Impedance measurements of the heterostructure were carried out at 500 Hz to 3 MHz from −3 V to 5 V with 50 mV steps. The capacitance (C) changes are presented separately in Fig. 2 a and 2b for low-medium and high frequencies to highlight the observed NC behavior. At frequencies in the 500 Hz-10 kHz range, the effect of the interface states is seen as an increase in C in both ( 0.60, 1.30) and ( 2.0, 4.0) voltage regions (see Fig. 2a). Above 10 kHz and up to 100 kHz, the increases in C are less pronounced. The rapid increase in C is due to the ability of the interface states and dipole/interfacial polarization to keep up with the AC signal at low-medium frequencies [32,33] At high frequencies, the changes in C are suppressed as the series resistance (Rs ) prevails in the accumulation region. Fig. 2b shows that at high frequencies, greater than 1.5 MHz, the PC is replaced by NC in forward biases. Schottky structures can exhibit NC behavior, depending on the distribution of interface states (Nss ) and/or Rs effects. The occurrence of NC behavior is still a controversial issue, but it can be simply classified into two categories: i) NC behavior observed at low frequencies is due to interface states, and ii) NC behavior observed at high frequencies is due to Rs
[34]. Although a high-level injection of minority carriers may also contribute to the NC behavior [35], we believe that the main effect in this study arises from Rs . This behavior is referred to as inductive behavior, in which the charges across the depletion region decrease rapidly, increasing the conductance (G). The G/ω behavior of the Au/La(OH)3NPs-PEIGQDsN/n-Si heterojunction is plotted in Fig. 2c. As for C, similar effects of Nss and Rs can be observed in the G/ω- V trends within the same voltage regions. The equation below can be used to obtain the resistance alteration of the structure (Ri ) in relation to the voltage as a function of frequency, C, and G/ω [36]:
(1)
R i
=
G m
G m 2
+
ω
C m
2
where Cm and Gm represent the measured C and G values for all voltage regions. However, the series resistance of the structure (Rs ) is obtained from the Ri values at high frequency and forward biases (accumulation region). In this case, Ri
≅
Rs . Fig. 2d shows that the double peaks observed in Ri at low-medium frequencies in the depletion region gradually disappear with increasing frequency, and the peak values shift towards higher forward biases. This is due to the re-alignment of Nss in that region with the changing AC signal [37].

On the other hand, in the accumulation region, Rs exhibit a strong frequency dependence. For instance, Rs at 5 V and 500 Hz is 630 Ω, but it decreases to 5.4 Ω at 1 MHz. The significant decrease in Rs , depending on frequency, causes the conductance (G) to increase rapidly. This is due to the charge recombination effect precluding the diffusion effect, which leads to a decrease in capacitance (C) to the point of reaching negative values [28,38].

In addition, reverse bias electrical parameters are discussed in this section. For this, the C -2 vs. V linear plots, which are inserted in Fig. 2b for 1 MHz, were used with the following equations:
(2)

- 2

)

E F

2

tan θ

2

N D
where VD , ND , EF , Wd , Em , ΔϕB , and ϕB stand for the diffusion potential, doping atom concentration, Fermi level, depletion layer width, maximum electric field, image force lowering, and Schottky barrier, respectively, and these values were listed in Table 1 for some frequencies.

Fig. 3 a and 3b show biaxial plots C and G/ω at two different high frequencies. At 1 MHz, changes in both C and G/ω increase in parallel in the depletion region, as common capacitive behavior. However, as we conducted measurements at higher frequencies, the increases in C values gradually diminished. Although there was a decrease in C, which reaches negative values at 3 MHz, the increase in G/ω indicates inductive behavior in Fig. 3b.

At this point, it is important to note that the observed NC at high frequencies may also be due to tunneling effects as a result of the high ND and narrow Wd . The Nss effects at the junction region include inhomogeneous-high ND and lattice defects of semiconductors, which can cause a rapid reduction in charge due to the migration of charge carriers into the metal through tunneling, especially due to the narrowing of Wd
[39,40]
. In the junction region, interface states (trap levels), localized at different energy levels, can affect the whereabouts of charge carriers in the bandgap. Thus, at certain frequency and voltage values, the capacitive or inductive behavior of the structure may depend on the distribution of Nss and the trapping times (lifetimes, τ of the charges. The parallel conductance method is a useful way to investigate the distributions of Nss and τ [41–43]. This method requires the following relationship:
(5)
G p
ω
=
ω
G m
C i
G m 2
+
ω 2
C i
-
C m
2
=
qA
N
ss
2 ω τ
ln
( 1 +
( ω τ )
2
)
where Ci , Cm , and Gm stand for the interlayer capacitance, measured C and G values, respectively. Fig. 4 a and 4b show the changes in parallel conductance (Gp/ω) calculated using Cm and Gm values obtained at specific voltage ranges from 500 Hz to 1 MHz. The resulting Gp/ω changes are directly related to Nss , as these changes (peaks) and the voltage regions (energy levels) corresponding to the changes result from Nss distributions. These figures demonstrate the dependence of Nss effects on voltage regions (0.85 – 1.25 V and 1.95 – 4.20 V) and frequency, as evidenced by the observed peak changes. The peak values (Gp /ω max) were used to substitute in
N
ss
=
G p
/ ω
max
/
0.402 q A
, which is the derivative form of equation (5). This resulted in obtaining Nss changes depending on the voltage, as seen in Fig. 4c and 4d.

In our previous study [7], it was found that the NC voltage region corresponds to the energy range where shallow traps are concentrated. In this study, NC was observed at around 1 V, which is the voltage region with a higher density of deep traps. It can be inferred that NC was not observed at low frequencies due to the high contribution of shallow traps to C and the insufficient narrowness of Wd to allow for tunneling. While the contribution of the traps to C decreased with increasing frequency, at the same time, the C curves experienced significant downward bending due to the dominant effect of Rs . Furthermore, since Wd narrows at high frequencies (due to increasing ND ), deep traps may facilitate charge tunneling (tunneling via interface states), leading to charge depletion at the junction and resulting in inductive behavior (NC) [44–48] ]. After all, we can conclude that the observed negative capacitance will likely stem from the contribution of both Rs and deep traps.

The energy levels at which charges are trapped may vary depending not only on their location relative to the Fermi level under the applied bias but also on the light incident on the surface. To clarify this situation, impedance measurements were conducted at high frequencies while the surface of the structure was illuminated at 100 mW/cm2 after the dark measurements. Fig. 5 a-d shows C and G/ω versus the voltage bias under illumination and in the dark, for different high frequencies.

It was observed that there were decreases in C and G/ω due to the effect of illumination at 1 MHz, where the structure exhibited capacitive behavior. This occurrence may have resulted from the recombination of the charges through the deep and/or shallow traps that were already filled in the depletion region under the influence of light [49–52]. At the frequencies of 2 MHz and 3 MHz, where inductive behavior was observed, light refilled the depleted trap levels, causing the NC to evolve into PC [49–52]. This phenomenon may occur due to the injection of photoexcited charges into deep traps or as a result of charges excited from deep traps to shallow traps or to the conduction band, which alters the junction region charge distribution [47,48]. These situations are illustrated in Fig. 6 a and 6b. In addition to these characteristics, the observed changes can also arise from the interlayer itself. Although the nature of interface states and the behavior of NC are not fully understood, they are important phenomena that require further investigation.

While NC behaviour was observed at low frequencies (1–5 kHz) in the lanthanide-free PEI-NGQDsN base diode, no NC behaviour was observed at any frequency and only PC behaviour was observed when lanthanide series Gd was doped into PEI-NGQDs. However, NC behaviour at high frequencies (above 1.5 MHz) was observed when La(OH)3NPs were doped into PEI NGQDs. Interestingly, at high frequencies (2 MHz and 3 MHz), the La doped sample showed a transition from NC to PC under illumination. It was concluded that NC is usually attributed to various surface states/interface traps, series resistance (Rs), and minority carrier injection. The NC phenomenon indicates that an increase in voltage gives rise to a decrease in the charge on the electrodes. The NC-PC behavior of doped and undoped PEIGQDsN structures is compared in Table 2 . These seminal investigations collectively underscore the nuanced interplay between dopants and frequency characteristics in GQDs-based diodes, elucidating the suppression or induction of NC behavior contingent upon doping elements such as Gd and La(OH)3NPs. The control of interfacial charges in such a heterostructure will be critical for NC devices.

In this study, the capacitance and conductance-voltage characteristics of green synthesized La(OH)3NPs doped PEIGQDsN nanocomposite-based heterojunction were examined over a broad frequency range from 500 Hz to 3 MHz under bias voltages between −3 V and 5 V at 300 K. The influence of illumination at 100 mW/cm2 on capacitance properties was also investigated. The findings suggest that the capacitance and conductance of the heterojunction have a remarkable frequency sensitivity, particularly evident at higher frequencies, notably above 1.5 MHz. At these frequencies, there is a transition from PC to NC under forward bias. As the frequency increases from low to high ranges, it becomes apparent that the structure initially showcasing positive capacitance undergoes a transition from positive to negative capacitive behavior. Additionally, under the illumination, at the frequencies of 2 MHz and 3 MHz, where inductive behavior has been seen, the light refilled the depleted trap levels, catalyzing the transition from NC to PC in forward bias. This makes the structure useful for high-frequencies. The results of this study are expected to have a significant impact on the understanding of electronic technology based on carbon-lanthanides and enable the creation of new hybrid functional materials for use in electronic or optoelectronic applications.

Aslıhan Anter: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Murat Ulusoy: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Barış Polat: Validation, Methodology. Mustafa Yıldız: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Antonio Di Bartolomeo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Jinshun Bi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Elif Orhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

This study was funded by the Scientific Research Council of Gazi University (BAP) grant number FGA-2022–8252, the REACT-EU PON Research and Innovation 2014–2020 project of the European Union, the Italian Ministerial Decree 1062/2021 and the University of Salerno, grant number ORSA235199.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Prof. M. Passacantando and Dr. E. Faella from the University of L’Aquila, Italy, for the XPS characterization of the interlayer materials used in this study.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Barati, M. Avatefi, N. B. Moghadam, S. Asghari, E. Ekrami, M. Mahmoudifard, J. Biomater. Appl. 2023, 37, 1137.
X. Tan, F. Zhou, W. Li, G. Ao, X. Xu, L. Yang, Coatings 2023, 13, 1956.
S. Deng, A. Fu, M. Junaid, Y. Wang, Q. Yin, C. Fu, L. Liu, D.-S. Su, W.-P. Bian, D.-S. Pei, Biomaterials 2019, 206, 61.
Y. Li, Y. Zhao, H. Cheng, Y. Hu, G. Shi, L. Dai, L. Qu, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 15.
A. Karatay, D. Erdener, C. Gürcan, E. A. Yildiz, A. Yilmazer, B. Boyacıoğlu, H. Unver, M. Yıldız, A. Elmali, J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem. 2022, 426, 113741.
Z. Berktaş, M. Yıldız, E. Seven, E. O. Orhan, Ş. Altındal, FlatChem 2022, 36, 100436.

B. A. Gunes, O. F. Kirlangic, M. Kilic, A. Sunguroglu, T. Ozgurtas, E. K. Sezginer, B. Boyacioglu, H. Unver, M. Yildiz, ACS omega 2024, 9, 13342.
